contention.topic: Air Pollution Control Advisory Committee appointment vetting standards
contention.description: A Penn Future representative used public comment to publicly criticize how past committee appointments had been handled, noting committees 'struggled with basics' including reaching quorum and following procedure. He urged council to apply three criteria: attendance/performance record, subject-matter expertise, and commitment to public health first — warning this should 'weigh heavily' in reappointment decisions.
contention.vote_split: Not a vote — public comment framing
contention.sides: Environmental advocates pushing for stricter accountability vs. standard appointment process.

public_comment_themes: Preview of 13809-26 ICE non-cooperation debate: strong majority in support from immigrant communities, legal professionals, faith leaders, and advocates citing 4th Amendment, financial liability, and community policing trust
Opposition to 13809-26 from residents citing public safety, federal law supremacy, and biblical duty to uphold civil government
Air Pollution Control Advisory Committee accountability: Penn Future urged rigorous appointment criteria including attendance, competence, and public health focus
Infrastructure/equity concerns: road clearing inequities in certain neighborhoods ('roads in southeast completely clear and dry while others impassable'), snow removal disparity
Policy wish list from individual speakers: minimum wage, state income tax repeal, social media age limits, marijuana taxation, sheriff department consolidation
McCandless Township non-cooperation with ICE mentioned as positive example for county to follow

meetings.title: Planning Commission — Bluff Townhomes Approved; Lot Consolidations in Polish Hill, East Liberty, and Perry South
key_topics: BDA-2025-12689: Approval of 3 three-story townhomes at 2037 Forbes Ave (Bluff/Uptown)
DCP-LOT-2026-00018: Herron Avenue consolidation of 34 parcels into 1 (Polish Hill)
DCP-LOT-2025-00309: Penn Avenue consolidation of 4 parcels into 1 (East Liberty)
DCP-LOT-2026-00025: Crispen Street lot line revision of 4 parcels (Perry South)
Farewell to departing commissioners: Blackwell (12 years), Woo (2 years), Dick (since 2015)
Director's report: Introduction to new comprehensive planning framework
summary_bullets: BDA-2025-12689 (2037 Forbes Ave) approved unanimously with two conditions: (1) DOMI permit review before Record of Zoning Approval, and (2) final construction plans reviewed by Zoning Administrator. The project adds three 3-story townhomes (multi-unit residential) in the Uptown Public Realm District B in the Bluff neighborhood, fronting Senica Street with rear courtyard and single-car garages. An existing duplex and church on site are retained.

DCP-LOT-2026-00018 (Herron Avenue consolidation, Polish Hill): Preliminary approval granted for consolidation of 34 parcels into one 93,360 sq ft lot with frontage on Herron Avenue. Several existing structures are slated for demolition. Final review scheduled for the March 10, 2026 meeting.

DCP-LOT-2025-00309 (Penn Avenue consolidation, East Liberty): Preliminary approval granted for consolidation of 4 parcels (102,599 sq ft) into one lot with frontage on Penn Avenue, currently a surface parking lot. Final review scheduled for March 10, 2026.

DCP-LOT-2026-00025 (Crispen Street lot line revision, Perry South): Preliminary approval for revision of 4 parcels including a large 232,287 sq ft agricultural parcel fronting Crispen Street. Final review scheduled for March 10, 2026.

Three long-serving commissioners bid farewell — Commissioners Blackwell (12 years), Woo (~2 years), Dick (since 2015), and Gam (~2 years) — at what was described as 'a moment in time' by the chair; replacements approved by City Council on March 10, 2026.
Correspondence noted regarding BDA-2025-12382 (Banksville) from neighbors Doug Seminara, Rose Marie Seminara, and Michelle Hickey Seminara, though that case was not on the day's agenda.
public_comment_themes: No public comment received on any agenda item
Neighbor correspondence submitted on un-agendaed Banksville case BDA-2025-12382

meetings.title: PWSA Board — Farewell to Chair Alex Shirley; Lead Service Line Progress, ALCOSAN Regionalization Update, Highland Reservoir Safety, and 6 Capital Resolutions
key_topics: Year-end 2025 financials: $253.6M receipts (105.6% of budget); debt service coverage 1.8x senior
2025 accomplishments: 7 miles water main replaced, 1,800+ lead service lines replaced, 60.5 miles sewer proposed for ALCOSAN transfer
Resolution: 2026 Catch Basin/Inlet Replacement Project — $1,491,182 to Independent Enterprises
Resolution: 2026 Manhole/Point Repair Project — $2,289,270 to Independent Enterprises
Resolution: Four Mile Run Stormwater Improvements amendment — $197,980 to MS Consultants
Resolution: Lead Service Line Replacement Contract A3 change order — $1,114,686 (Mount Washington)
Resolution: 2023 Large Diameter Sewer Rehab change order — $85,750 (Sheraden/Esplen, 100+ ft missing invert)
Resolution: Condemnation/purchase at 253 Britain Street (Breitton Landslide Remediation)
Farewell to outgoing Chair Alex Shirley; Erica named interim chair; officer elections set for February
bills_mentioned: Resolution 4/2026 — Lead Service Line change order
summary_bullets: 2025 year-end financials: Receipts totaled $253.6M (105.6% of budget due to higher-than-expected revenue); operating expenditures $178.2M (101.7% of budget); capital expenditures $28.3M (64.4% of budget, well below target); debt service $88.6M; senior debt service coverage ratio of 1.8x (exceeds metrics).

2025 highlights reported by CEO Will: 7 miles of water main replaced; 500+ valves, 100+ hydrants; 1,800+ public lead service lines replaced (out of 14,000+ total program); Highland Reservoir pump station renovation; green infrastructure maintenance brought fully in-house for first time; $800M in Pennvest loans and $120M in grants secured; worker apprenticeship program and 10 career fairs.

ALCOSAN regionalization: Board previously approved (January) resolution to continue discussions on transfer of ~60.5 miles of sewer pipe and 1,630 manholes (≥10 inches diameter with intermunicipal flow) to ALCOSAN. 2026 work plan includes asset inventory/defect review, permit research, and cost discussions.

Resolution for 253 Britain Street (Breitton Landslide, Brookline/South Hills): Board authorized condemnation or purchase of condemned house at 253 Britain Street to enable landslide remediation. Landslide occurred fall 2024 due to combination of inadequate stormwater management and a PWSA water main leak. PWSA is taking lead on remediation while seeking cost-sharing agreement with the City.
Highland Reservoir (Highland Park): Update on slope repair project at Highland 2 Reservoir, a high-hazard dam requiring slide repair. Earlier board request for location update fulfilled.

Lead Service Line Contract A3 change order (Mount Washington): $1,114,686 change order approved because verification digs revealed 30% lead service lines vs. the estimated 25%, requiring more replacement work than originally contracted.

Outgoing Chair Alex Shirley received praise from Jim Turner (public comment) and the full board; Shirley was described as having transformed PWSA from a 'failed organization atop crumbling infrastructure' to a model utility. Interim Chair Erica to lead until February officer elections.
public_comment_themes: Public praise for outgoing Chair Alex Shirley and PWSA's institutional transformation
Board member emphasis on Independent Enterprises contractor conduct reflecting on PWSA and the city

meetings.title: Planning Commission — Duquesne University Academic Building Approved; Squirrel Hill Subdivision
key_topics: BDA-2024-08149: Approval of 5-story 80,000 sq ft Duquesne University Rangos School academic building at 1206 Forbes Ave (Bluff)
DCP-LOT-2026-00003: Major subdivision of 5429 Dunmoyle Street into two lots (Squirrel Hill North)
Introduction of new Zoning Administrator Carolyn Rristau
summary_bullets: BDA-2024-08149 (1206 Forbes Ave) approved unanimously (all 6 commissioners) with conditions: Duquesne University's 5-story, 80,000 sq ft Rangos School of Health Sciences building (classrooms, labs, clinical health care, offices, ground-floor clinic) on Forbes Ave. The project includes a publicly accessible open space plaza, 10 ft sidewalks, and street trees along Forbes Ave and McGee St.

The building provides 53% ground-floor transparency along Forbes Avenue — slightly below the 60% recommended in the Institutional Master Plan (IMP) — due to health care/educational privacy needs. This was accepted by the Commission.

93 trees to be removed and replaced at a 1:1 ratio coordinated with the city's forestry department. Project reviewed at Contextual Design Advisory Panel October 14, 2025, and community organizations on October 20, 2025 (largely supportive).

DCP-LOT-2026-00003 (5429 Dunmoyle Street, Squirrel Hill North): Major Subdivision 1 approved — splits one parcel into two lots (Lot 1: 12,174 sq ft; Lot 2: 7,482 sq ft, both fronting Dunmoyle Street). Approved 5-0-1 (Commissioner Gam abstained).
Director's Report: Introduction of new Zoning Administrator Carolyn Rristau, described as having a background in Pittsburgh zoning, consulting, and process improvements.
public_comment_themes: No public comment received on any agenda item

news.headline: O'Connor says Pittsburgh budget short some $30 million in key areas
news.summary: Pittsburgh Mayor Corey O'Connor announced that the city's 2026 budget requires an immediate overhaul after his team identified $30–$40 million in underestimated costs since taking office in January, including shortfalls in employee health care, retiree health care, bridge upkeep, fuel, and fleet repair. He pledged to revise the budget starting in March by examining unfilled positions and trimming spending, while ruling out further tax hikes, layoffs, or cuts to core services like violence prevention or affordable housing. The announcement followed an unaudited report showing the city ended 2025 with an $8.6 million deficit and spent more than $20 million over budget on overtime.

news.headline: Pittsburgh will reopen its 2026 budget to 'address serious concerns'
news.summary: Mayor Corey O'Connor announced Thursday that he will reopen the city's already-adopted 2026 budget to address serious concerns, a rare move typically reserved for contract adjustments. The decision follows Controller Rachel Heistler's warnings that Pittsburgh is overspending and under-collecting revenue, and comes after council raised property taxes 20% in late 2025. O'Connor said the city is projecting a $30–$40 million gap and will examine every contract and unfilled position to close the deficit without new taxes or layoffs.

news.headline: Post-Penguins planning for Pittsburgh's Lower Hill begins
news.summary: The Urban Redevelopment Authority of Pittsburgh formally took over Lower Hill development rights from the Pittsburgh Penguins and voted to seek proposals for approximately seven acres between Bedford and Centre Avenues to build affordable and market-rate housing on what is currently surface parking. The URA will also formalize site control of 1.75 acres at Centre Avenue and Crawford Street to advance the Bedford Dwellings redevelopment, a mixed-income housing project funded by a federal Choice Neighborhoods grant. Nearly $1 million in reinvestment funds was approved for four Hill District projects including African Queens Apartments, Big Tom's Barbershop rehabilitation at 2178 Centre, the Rhythm Square artist studios at 2239 Centre, and a mixed-use building at 2932 Wylie Ave.

news.headline: Nobody's building offices in Pittsburgh. Housing may benefit.
news.summary: Pittsburgh's commercial development has slowed sharply since the pandemic, with approximately 17% of citywide office space vacant, prompting developers like Oxford Development to shift planned office buildings in the Strip District to residential use. Downtown Pittsburgh is exploring publicly funded office-to-residential conversions to add foot traffic and affordability, with conversions planned at locations including 901-903 Liberty Ave. and 120 Cecil Way. Regional data shows nearly 6,000 residential building permits were issued last year, up from pre-pandemic levels, suggesting housing demand is outpacing office development.

news.headline: State officials pledge nearly $3.5M to Pittsburgh Downtown Partnership to support new businesses
news.summary: The Pennsylvania Department of Community and Economic Development is directing nearly $3.5 million in tax credits over six years to the Pittsburgh Downtown Partnership through the Neighborhood Assistance Program. The funding will support street cleaning services, business recruitment, and the creation of a new dining district at Market Square, and will expand a rent abatement program aimed at bringing 30 new businesses downtown. Partnership leaders framed the investment as critical to reversing downtown's post-pandemic struggles.

news.headline: PRT to Hold Public Meeting on Belasco Station Project
news.summary: Pittsburgh Regional Transit announced an in-person public meeting for March 26 at St. Catherine of Siena Church at 1810 Belasco Avenue regarding planned upgrades to the Belasco Light Rail Station on the Red Line in the Beechview neighborhood. The meeting will cover existing conditions and updates to improvement plans first presented in 2020; the station sits on Broadway Avenue where light rail vehicles run parallel to vehicle traffic. The station is not currently ADA accessible, and PRT will provide ASL interpretation at the meeting.

news.headline: Shaler Area preparing to borrow $7.5M to pay for capital projects including roof at middle school
news.summary: The Shaler Area School District is preparing to borrow $7.5 million to fund a range of capital projects, including roof replacement at the middle school and safety and auditorium upgrades at Shaler Area High School projected at approximately $100,000. The district has a $1.3 million state reimbursement grant supporting these infrastructure repairs. The borrowing reflects broader pressures on suburban school districts across Allegheny County to maintain aging facilities while managing tight budgets.

news.headline: "Growth as a priority:" Two O'Connor officials share their business development plans for Pittsburgh
news.summary: Pittsburgh economic development officials Steve Wray and Mary McKinney Flaherty outlined the O'Connor administration's strategies for business recruitment, permitting reform, and economic growth at a NAIOP Pittsburgh panel discussion. The officials described a pro-growth philosophy centered on streamlining development approvals and attracting new businesses and residents, characterizing growth as the administration's top priority. The presentation reflected O'Connor's broader campaign to reposition Pittsburgh as open for business after years of developer frustration under the prior Gainey administration.

news.headline: Sara Innamorato: Safe, affordable housing for every county resident
news.summary: Allegheny County Executive Sara Innamorato outlined her HOUSING for All framework, describing rising costs, stagnant wages, aging housing stock, absentee landlords, and private equity as drivers of a housing crisis requiring a coordinated countywide response. She highlighted the county's Housing Investment Fund—designed to raise $50–$100 million—and the 500 in 500 Initiative that has housed more than 620 people exiting shelter. Innamorato called for preservation of existing affordable housing through home repair and lead remediation, alongside anti-displacement strategies to protect vulnerable communities.

news.headline: After Penguins exit, Pittsburgh moves to revive Lower Hill with housing proposals
news.summary: After decades of stagnation on the 28-acre former Civic Arena site, the URA opened a public comment period on plans that include a 140-unit apartment complex with half the units designated affordable and a broader request for proposals covering another seven acres. The push follows the expiration of the Pittsburgh Penguins' development rights in October 2025, and officials also announced that all future property tax revenue from the site will be reinvested locally. Some Hill District residents remain cautious after decades of past delays and displacement.

news.headline: New development envisioned for Westinghouse site in Churchill
news.summary: Commonwealth Commercial Real Estate presented early renderings of Legacy Crossings, a proposed walkable mixed-use development on the former Westinghouse Research and Technology Park in Churchill Borough, to the Churchill Borough Council. Plans include wholesale and retail grocery stores, restaurants, a gas station, parkland, a town center, medical offices, and residential units, with an emphasis on non-auto-centric design. Churchill Borough is also considering zoning amendments to block data centers, vape shops, and tattoo parlors following past community opposition to an Amazon distribution center on the site.

news.headline: URA to Advance Lower Hill Redevelopment Process; Board Set to Vote on $930,000 to Support Four Transformative Development Projects in the Greater Hill District
news.summary: The URA formally announced it will issue a Request for Proposals for the redevelopment of 6.82 acres on the Lower Hill site (Blocks A and C) as surface parking is converted into affordable and/or market-rate housing with potential mixed-use and commercial components. The board also set a vote to approve $930,000 in grants from the Greater Hill District Neighborhood Reinvestment Fund to four community projects: African Queens Apartments ($250K), Big Tom's Barbershop rehabilitation ($240K), Rhythm Square artist studios ($250K), and a Communion LLC mixed-use building at 2932 Wylie Ave ($190K). Public comment on the draft RFP will be open from March 12 through April 7, 2026.

news.headline: More than 50 Pittsburgh Regional Transit bus routes to be detoured for St. Patrick's Day 2026
news.summary: Pittsburgh Regional Transit announced that more than 50 bus routes will be detoured on Saturday, March 14, for the city's annual St. Patrick's Day Parade and related events, with most detours running from 7 a.m. to 2 p.m. and some lasting until 5 p.m. when police are expected to reopen streets. Because several bus routes will be unable to reach parts of Downtown, riders will be able to disembark and transfer for free to light-rail cars at Station Square or Penn Station, the bus and rail hub along the Martin Luther King Jr. East Busway. PRT will operate a free rail shuttle between Penn Station and Steel Plaza to accommodate riders during the event.

news.headline: Hazelwood Green: Attracting tech jobs, businesses, economic activity — and homes for new residents
news.summary: Hazelwood Green, redeveloped by RIDC from the former LTV Steel mill site, is becoming a nationally recognized hub for robotics, artificial intelligence, and life sciences with the official opening of Carnegie Mellon University's $100 million, 150,000-square-foot Robotics Innovation Center adjacent to Mill 19. The University of Pittsburgh's BioForge building, spanning 185,000 square feet, is nearing completion and will be anchored by ElevateBio to advance biomedical manufacturing. Residential projects and a Pittsburgh Steelers-backed community sports facility are also under construction on the site.

news.headline: Rep. Summer Lee Presents $3M Check to Support Affordable Housing in Allegheny County
news.summary: Congresswoman Summer L. Lee joined Allegheny County Executive Sara Innamorato and Lieutenant Governor Austin Davis at the Avenue Apartments in Braddock to present a $3 million Community Project Funding investment for affordable housing initiatives across Allegheny County. The funding aims to expand access to safe and affordable housing for working families, seniors, and children in the face of rising rents and homelessness. Officials emphasized the need for coordinated federal, state, and local efforts, pointing to the county's HOUSING for All executive order signed by Innamorato in February 2026.

news.headline: Pittsburgh Police, Fire, and EMS Respond to Shooting in Larimer
news.summary: Pittsburgh Police Zone 5, Pittsburgh Fire, and Pittsburgh EMS responded to the unit block of Auburn Street in the Larimer neighborhood after two ShotSpotter alerts detected thirteen rounds fired. Officers located an adult female inside a residence with a gunshot wound to her left thigh; she was transported to a local hospital in stable condition, and bullet damage was also found at the residence. The Mobile Crime Unit responded to process evidence, and Violent Crime Unit detectives are leading the ongoing investigation.

news.headline: O'Connor unveils overhaul of Pittsburgh's permitting process
news.summary: Mayor Corey O'Connor unveiled a three-phase overhaul of Pittsburgh's permitting and zoning systems, fulfilling a key campaign promise to make the city more development-friendly. The plan includes immediately expediting building permits, then revising the zoning code to remove obsolete sections, simplify height requirements, reduce zoning districts, and increase allowed heights in Urban Neighborhood Commercial districts. A third phase will modernize the certification process through online portals, with O'Connor noting that New York City issues permits in four to five weeks while Pittsburgh cannot.

news.headline: Pittsburgh launches zoning and permit overhaul
news.summary: Mayor Corey O'Connor announced a three-phase plan to ease building rules, spur housing growth, and streamline permit approvals, fulfilling a major campaign commitment to shift the city from a 'culture of no' to one of growth. Phase one immediately expedites building permits; phase two revises the zoning code to increase heights in Urban Neighborhood Commercial districts including the Baum Corridor in the East End; phase three consolidates applications through searchable online portals. O'Connor specified no citywide inclusionary zoning is planned, but targeted IZ near transit lines or in density-bonus areas remains possible.

news.headline: Pittsburgh colleges expand their footprint to meet demand
news.summary: Pittsburgh's major universities—Point Park, Duquesne, Pitt, and Carnegie Mellon—are acquiring and repurposing buildings across several neighborhoods to meet growing enrollment demand, including Point Park's plans for the former YWCA on Wood Street in Downtown and Pitt's BioForge facility at Hazelwood Green. Despite most university properties being tax-exempt, officials argue the expansions generate economic benefits through construction jobs, neighborhood vibrancy, and some taxable development. Officials from the universities note expansion projects are creating new space for employees, supporting private investment, and contributing to neighborhood safety.

news.headline: University Line Construction to Impact Traffic Patterns Along Fifth and Forbes Avenues Through the End of April
news.summary: Pittsburgh Regional Transit announced that Phase Two University Line construction activity along Fifth Avenue between Bellefield and Robinson Streets and along Forbes Avenue between Jumonville and Birmingham Bridge will impact traffic patterns in Oakland and Uptown through the end of April. Beginning March 13, crews will mill, patch, and pave roadways and install new sidewalks, curbs, and ADA-compliant ramps; the bus stop at Fifth Avenue and Brenham Street will be taken out of service. Future phases will add PRTX stations, dedicated red bus lanes, upgraded traffic signals, and pedestrian safety improvements along the $291 million bus rapid transit corridor connecting Downtown, Uptown, and Oakland.

news.headline: Pittsburgh man charged in shooting death of sister in Greenfield neighborhood
news.summary: Aaron Charles Martin, 27, was charged with criminal homicide and related offenses following the fatal shooting of his sister, 26-year-old Charlee Martin, at an apartment complex on the 700 block of Montclair Street in Pittsburgh's Greenfield neighborhood in February 2026. Police found Charlee Martin dead inside the apartment after responding to a domestic dispute call, with multiple bullet casings around her body; Aaron Martin was found in a bedroom and taken into custody following a brief struggle. Additional charges including firearms violations and abuse of a corpse were filed after he was cleared from a hospital.

news.headline: Rising cost to build: Pittsburgh developers fighting challenges to build more multifamily in a tricky business climate
news.summary: Pittsburgh multifamily developers are navigating mounting challenges from higher labor, financing, and construction costs that are outpacing rent growth across the region, forcing builders including Oxford Development to pursue smaller, more financially viable projects. The cost pressures are constraining the housing supply pipeline at a time when demand remains strong and the city is pushing for more residential construction. Developers say creative financing strategies and public-private partnerships are increasingly necessary to make the numbers work on new projects.

news.headline: PRT's second draft of bus route changes increases frequency on core routes
news.summary: Pittsburgh Regional Transit unveiled the Bus Line Refresh proposal, an updated redesign of the county's bus network shaped by feedback from more than 16,000 riders, operators, and community members that maintains familiar routes while improving frequency and reliability. Key improvements include 20% more Saturday service and 18% more Sunday service, 43 routes operating every 30 minutes or better (up from 27), and new one-seat rides to Oakland from communities including McCandless, Ross, Dormont, Brentwood, Millvale, Mount Lebanon, Carrick, and the West Busway. New routes include O35 Mt. Lebanon–Dormont, O45 Carrick–East Liberty, and O9 North Hills–Oakland; if approved by the PRT board this fall, implementation would begin in 2027.

news.headline: Bill to bar Allegheny County cooperation with ICE advances to council vote
news.summary: Allegheny County Council's public safety committee voted 4-3 to advance a bill that would prohibit county employees from cooperating with U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement, bar ICE from housing detainees in the county jail without a judicial warrant, and restrict federal immigration officers from accessing county databases. The bill, sponsored by Councilwoman Bethany Hallam and colleagues, sparked about an hour of debate over existing policies, potential loss of federal funding under the Trump administration, and enforceability. Approximately 50 residents attended the meeting, applauding the committee's decision to advance it to a full council vote.

news.headline: PRT Bus Line Refresh: Familiar Routes, Stronger Connections — Updated Bus Network Proposal Launched
news.summary: Pittsburgh Regional Transit launched the Bus Line Refresh proposal, an evolution of the 2023 Bus Line Redesign effort that prioritizes increasing frequency and reliability on existing routes rather than a wholesale overhaul, following feedback from more than 16,000 riders and community members. The proposal reinvests more than 10% of service hours from low-ridership segments into higher-demand areas, with 195,000 residents in equity communities gaining 30-minute service—a 28% increase—and 380,000 additional jobs accessible at 30-minute frequency. PRT CEO Katharine Kelleman said more than 60 public meetings and pop-ups will be held across Allegheny County before required public hearings; if board-approved in fall 2026, implementation would begin in 2027.

developments.title: Kelly Street Rezoning
developments.description: The Urban Redevelopment Authority (URA) is proposing to rezone three parcels in the Larimer neighborhood from R1D-L (Residential Single Unit Detached – Low Density) to UI (Urban Industrial). The site is bounded by Kelly Street to the north and Formosa Way to the south. Initial plans from the property owner propose a new multiunit residential building on the site, contingent on the rezoning. The UI district allows up to 60 ft height (4 stories), 3:1 FAR, and greater building densities.
developments.key_details: Proposed by URA; rezoning from R1D-L to UI; future multiunit residential building planned; requires Planning Commission and City Council hearings; Development Activities Meeting held with Homewood Community Development Collaborative

developments.title: Lower Hill District Housing RFP
developments.description: The Urban Redevelopment Authority (URA) is releasing a Request for Proposals (RFP) to redevelop 6.82 acres of URA-owned land on portions of Block A and Block C on the Lower Hill site, currently used as surface parking. The RFP will seek developers for affordable and/or market-rate housing, with additional consideration for mixed-use and commercial components. This is part of the broader 28-acre Lower Hill redevelopment.
developments.address: Lower Hill District, Block A and Block C
developments.key_details: 6.82 acres URA-owned land; public comment period open March 12 – April 7, 2026; URA Board vote on April 9, 2026; also includes Bedford Dwellings/Hill District mixed-income housing project (Trek Development Group + HACP) on 1.75 acres; funded in part by HUD Choice Neighborhoods Implementation Grant; $930,000 in Greater Hill District Neighborhood Reinvestment Fund grants also approved for four neighborhood projects (African Queens Apartments, Big Tom's Barbershop, Rhythm Square, Herron and Wylie Project)

developments.title: Pittsburgh Comprehensive Plan
developments.description: Pittsburgh's first-ever integrated citywide comprehensive plan, guiding growth and development across all 90 neighborhoods over a 20-year horizon. The plan will address future land use, zoning, climate action, housing, transportation, equity, and neighborhood planning. It must be approved by the Planning Commission and City Council.
developments.key_details: Two-year planning process; community-led engagement with consultant team; guided by 'Just Transition' and climate justice principles; covers 8 themes: welcoming community, safe city, youth, economic opportunity, connected city, healthy residents, aging in place, thriving neighborhoods; Department of City Planning lead

developments.title: Implementing the Housing Needs Assessment – Zoning Reforms
developments.description: The Department of City Planning is implementing a suite of land-use zoning code reforms to increase housing options and affordability across all incomes. Completed reforms include minimum lot size reduction citywide (legislation 2025-1579 now in effect). Ongoing work includes accessory dwelling units (ADUs), inclusionary zoning, parking reform, and a comprehensive residential rezoning overhaul anticipated in late 2025/2026.
developments.key_details: Minimum lot size legislation 2025-1579 passed and in effect citywide; future phases include comprehensive residential zoning code overhaul; inclusionary zoning (IZ) overlay currently active in Lawrenceville, Bloomfield, Polish Hill, portions of Oakland; parking reform complete; ADU expansion in progress

developments.title: Mayor O'Connor Permitting Process Reform & Zoning Code Overhaul
developments.description: Mayor Corey O'Connor announced a three-phase overhaul of the city's permitting and development operations on March 9, 2026. Phase 1 includes 20+ immediate actions: a 'fast lane' for quick permit approvals, more over-the-counter permits, triage by project size, AI-assisted application review, and virtual inspections. Phase 2 revises the zoning code (simplifying height requirements, updating UNC districts). Phase 3 consolidates online portals.
developments.key_details: Announced March 9, 2026; executive order issued on mayor's first day requiring 60-day departmental review; first comprehensive zoning code rewrite in decades; Phase 1 actions being implemented Q1-Q2 2026; no citywide mandatory inclusionary zoning planned; targeted IZ near transit lines possible; DCP lead

developments.title: California Ave. Bridge Rehabilitation Project
developments.description: Rehabilitation of the California Avenue Bridge (Robert McAfee Bridge), which connects Brighton Heights and Marshall Shadeland and is a key roadway linking the North Hills to North Shore and Downtown. The bridge was built in 1928, rehabilitated in 1982, and is now rated 'Fair.' The project is currently in preliminary engineering (2024-2025), with final design planned for 2026-2027 and construction in 2028.
developments.key_details: Bridge built 1928; eligible for National Register of Historic Places (Criterion C); community meeting held July 24, 2025; federal FHWA and PennDOT District 11-0 cooperation; consultants: MS Consultants (prime), Azalea Group, Monaloh Basin Engineers, GEO-Mechanics, Tri-State Design, Traffic Planning and Design; Timeline: Preliminary Engineering 2024-2025 → Final Design 2026-2027 → Construction 2028

developments.title: Frankstown Avenue Signal Improvements
developments.description: DOMI is making traffic signal improvements along Frankstown Avenue from Murtland Avenue to Blackadore Street in Homewood. The $4.3 million federally funded project (CMAQ grant) includes signal replacements at 4 intersections, signal removal/conversion to all-way stop at 2 intersections, curb extensions, ADA ramps, high visibility crosswalks, raised intersections, and speed humps. Construction began January 2026.
developments.address: Frankstown Avenue, Murtland Ave to Blackadore St
developments.key_details: Federal CMAQ grant of ~$4.3 million; construction began January 2026; 9 intersections in scope; part of 2022 Homewood Mobility Plan; DOMI lead; signal replacements at Murtland, Lang, Homewood, Collier, Brushton, Blackadore; signal removal at Braddock; all-way stops at Sterrett and Braddock; ADA improvements citywide

developments.title: Liberty Avenue Highway Safety Improvement Project
developments.description: A Highway Safety Improvement Program (HSIP) project to update roadway infrastructure along Liberty Avenue in the Strip District, improving vehicular and pedestrian safety. Includes full signal replacements at 16th, 21st, 25th, 26th, 28th, and 31st Streets, a new signal at 27th St, pedestrian bump-outs, refuge islands, improved lane widths, and transit improvements including widened bus stop sidewalks.
developments.address: Liberty Avenue, Strip District (17th St to 32nd St)
developments.key_details: HSIP (Federal Highway Safety Improvement Program) funded; full signal replacement at 6 intersections; new signal at 27th St; RRFB conversion at 17th St; part of Strip District Mobility Plan; also coordinates with Penn Avenue and Smallman Street corridor; transit signal priority under consideration

developments.title: Brighton Heights, California & Termon Intersection Improvement
developments.description: A study and design project to improve the intersection at Brighton Heights Boulevard, California Avenue, and Termon Avenue — a key gateway linking Brighton Heights to Ohio River Blvd (SR 65) and the McKees Rocks Bridge. From 2020–2024, nine crashes were reported at this intersection, four causing serious injury. The project will redesign signal timing, add protected turn phases, and improve pedestrian crossing conditions.
developments.address: Brighton Heights Blvd / California Ave / Termon Ave
developments.key_details: 9 crashes 2020-2024 (4 serious injury, 1 pedestrian injury); PRT Route 13 serves this intersection (~32 boardings/weekday); dominant crash type: 'angle' (turning/yielding behavior); project will produce implementable design and cost estimates but does not guarantee funding or immediate construction; DOMI lead

developments.title: Penn Plaza Block B (Pennley Park South)
developments.description: The final phase of the overall Pennley Park South site master plan at the former Penn Plaza site in East Liberty. Developer LG Realty Advisors, Inc. is advancing development plans for Block B following completion of Block A (Whole Foods, 2022) and Block C (renovated Enright Park). City Planning and Council District 9 are facilitating community engagement given the history of mass displacement of former Penn Plaza residents.
developments.key_details: Developer: LG Realty Advisors, Inc.; privately-owned development (City role is zoning compliance review and engagement facilitation); Block A (Whole Foods) completed 2022; Block C (Enright Park renovation) complete; Block B is final phase; Council District 9 (Councilman Khari Mosley) involved; community questionnaire process underway

developments.title: Bloomfield Bridge Rehabilitation
developments.description: The City is rehabilitating the Bloomfield Bridge (completed 1986), which links the Bloomfield neighborhood to Downtown and Oakland. Noted as the largest deck-area bridge in Pittsburgh, spanning over the East Busway and railroad lines. The planned work will address pier deterioration and deck issues through substructure repair, bearing cleaning, spot painting, new deck overlay, and concrete approach repairs. Maintenance work on expansion joint seals was completed fall 2025.
developments.key_details: Largest deck-area bridge in Pittsburgh; spans East Busway and railroad lines; emergency maintenance (expansion joint seals) completed fall 2025; full rehabilitation in planning phase; DOMI lead; work scope: substructure repair, bearing cleaning, steel spot painting, deck overlay, concrete approach repairs

developments.title: Serpentine Drive Wall Repair & Access Conversion
developments.description: Restoration of the historic retaining wall along Serpentine Drive in Schenley Park, where an 80-foot section collapsed in 2019. The project will repair approximately 265 feet of masonry wall with drainage improvements. Upon reopening, DOMI proposes Serpentine Drive be restricted to bike and pedestrian access only, eliminating motor vehicle access at both Barlett St and E Circuit Road ends.
developments.key_details: Wall collapsed in 2019; ~265 feet of masonry wall replacement/repair; architectural treatment to replicate original stone masonry aesthetics; proposes closure to motor vehicles permanently; 60-day public comment period ended February 29, 2024; bike/pedestrian-only access planned; Council District 5

developments.title: Warrington Recreation Center Renovation
developments.description: Comprehensive renovation of the Warrington Recreation Center, a long-standing community hub. Interior improvements include a new recording studio, refreshed basketball and workout areas, warming kitchen, and an elevator for full ADA accessibility. Exterior improvements add a new ADA-accessible playground, outdoor basketball court, and improved site durability.
developments.key_details: Funded by Redevelopment Assistance Capital Program (RACP) grants and local dollars; key features: recording studio, elevator (ADA), new playground, outdoor basketball court; Department of Public Works lead; project update posted January 2026

developments.title: Vision Zero – Traffic Safety Program
developments.description: Pittsburgh's citywide program to eliminate all traffic fatalities and serious injuries, adopted formally in 2024 following the city's Complete Streets Policy (2016). The program coordinates multiple agencies and roadway users using a safe systems approach focused on roadway design, education, and policy. It encompasses multiple active sub-projects including the Neighborhood Traffic Calming Program, Safe Routes to School, automated enforcement, and complete streets implementation.
developments.key_details: Formally adopted 2024; Mayor Gainey's 2024 budget nearly doubled traffic safety capital investment; sub-programs include: Neighborhood Traffic Calming, Safe Routes to School, Automated Red Light Enforcement (ARLE), bike lane enforcement cameras, Complete Streets; DOMI lead

developments.title: Vending Program Rules & Regulations
developments.description: The City of Pittsburgh updated its mobile vending program through City Council legislation passed November 18, 2025 (Ordinances 34 and 35). The new rules and regulations governing the permitting process were anticipated to be published on March 12, 2026, upon which the new vending program would fully take effect. The project team was seeking public feedback on draft rules and regulations in early 2026.
developments.key_details: Legislation passed November 18, 2025; signed by Mayor Gainey November 19, 2025; Ordinances 34 and 35; amended Zoning Code and Chapter 719 (Vendors and Peddlers); final rules/regulations publication target: March 12, 2026; PLI (Department of Permits, Licenses & Inspections) lead; covers stationary and mobile vendors in public right-of-way

developments.title: Historic Preservation Program
developments.description: Ongoing program by the Department of City Planning to protect and maintain significant landmarks throughout Pittsburgh. Guided by PreservePGH (Cultural Heritage Plan, 2012), the program includes establishing clear landmark nomination processes, Certificates of Appropriateness, and historic district design guidelines. Recent activity includes new design guidelines contracts for the Mexican War Streets and Manchester Historic Districts (funded March 2026).
developments.key_details: Guided by PreservePGH (2012); ongoing Architectural Inventory Project (surveys since 2014); new design guidelines contract approved March 2026 for Mexican War Streets and Manchester Historic Districts (Evolve Environment::Architecture, $37,500); includes Hill District Pilot Process for Reuse/Reinvestment; DCP lead

developments.title: South Side Park Project
developments.description: Ongoing improvements to South Side Park, including Phase 1 renovations involving stormwater infrastructure (r-tanks maintained by Pittsburgh Water) and a new public art project funded by the Percent for Art program. City Council passed a resolution authorizing Pittsburgh Water access to the park to maintain stormwater r-tanks in March 2026.
developments.key_details: Phase 1 ongoing; stormwater r-tanks installed in coordination with Pittsburgh Water (PWSA); public art project funded by Percent for Art program; City Council Resolution 2026-0177 authorized Pittsburgh Water maintenance access March 2026; Department of Public Works lead

developments.title: Safe Routes to School Program
developments.description: The Safe Routes to School (SRTS) program promotes students biking and walking to school through education, encouragement, and engineering safety improvements. Goals include educating school-aged children in safe walking and biking practices, encouraging active transportation, improving safety around crosswalks, signage, and sidewalks near schools, and partnering with community organizations citywide.
developments.key_details: Ongoing annual program; DOMI lead; combines engineering improvements (crosswalks, signage, sidewalks) with education and encouragement; part of Vision Zero safe systems approach; updated January 2026

developments.title: Pittsburgh Regional Disparity Study
developments.description: The City of Pittsburgh is conducting a regional disparity study to understand the effects of race, ethnicity, and gender on the ability to do business throughout the city. The study examines whether there is disparity in the city's contracting and procurement, and will inform future Minority Business Enterprise (MBE) and Women Business Enterprise (WBE) program policies.
developments.key_details: Citywide procurement disparity analysis; examines race, ethnicity, and gender-based disparities in city contracting; will inform MBE/WBE program policies; findings will guide equitable contracting requirements; Mayor's Office lead

developments.title: Allegheny Riverfront Park Restoration
developments.description: A $6.3 million restoration of Allegheny Riverfront Park's upper level between the Sister Bridges (Roberto Clemente, Andy Warhol, Rachel Carson) in Downtown Pittsburgh. Work includes replacing pavement, new site grading for accessibility, new amenities, refurbishing light posts and railings, tree pruning, soil amendments, and perennial planting. Led by Riverlife in partnership with the City and Pittsburgh Cultural Trust; original designer Michael Van Valkenburgh Associates involved.
developments.address: Allegheny Riverfront Park (between 6th, 7th, and 9th Street Bridges)
developments.key_details: Total project cost: $6.3 million; partners: Riverlife, City of Pittsburgh, Pittsburgh Cultural Trust; designer: Michael Van Valkenburgh Associates + LaQuatra Bonci Associates; construction anticipated to begin 2024 (per earlier plans, may be underway); community meetings held December 2022 and February 2023; part of Riverlife 'Completing the Loop' vision

developments.title: Westinghouse Park Master Plan
developments.description: A community-led master planning process for Westinghouse Park (10.2 acres) in Point Breeze North and Homewood, formerly the estate of George and Marguerite Westinghouse. The plan incorporates gathering space, recreation amenities, ecological restoration, and stormwater management. The final plan has been published; implementation is the next phase.
developments.key_details: 10.2-acre park; partnership between Westinghouse Park 2nd Century Coalition and Point Breeze North Development Corporation; funded by URA Neighborhood Initiative program grant and private funder; final master plan published; implementation phase upcoming; historically significant (Westinghouse estate 'Solitude')
